C:
Use server-side rendering — and for a content-heavy marketing site specifically, prefer its static flavor (SSG / prerendering at build time) over per-request SSR. Render to HTML on the server; only hydrate the few interactive bits that genuinely need it.

The reasoning:

**SEO is the deciding factor.** Marketing sites live or die by organic search and link previews. With client-side rendering, crawlers and social scrapers receive a near-empty HTML shell and have to execute JavaScript to see your content. Google can do this but does it inconsistently and on a delayed second-pass; most other crawlers and the Open Graph scrapers behind Slack, iMessage, LinkedIn, and X don't run JS at all. Server-rendered HTML puts your copy, headings, and meta tags in the initial response where every crawler reads them reliably.

**First paint is faster, which converts better.** Content-heavy pages are mostly text and images — exactly what HTML is good at delivering immediately. SSR/SSG shows real content on the first byte; CSR shows nothing until the JS bundle downloads, parses, and fetches data, then renders. On marketing pages every extra second of blank screen measurably costs you bounce rate, and Core Web Vitals (LCP in particular) feed back into search ranking.

**The content is the right shape for it.** Marketing content changes when someone edits the CMS, not on every request. That means you can build pages to static HTML and serve them from a CDN — cheapest, fastest, and most resilient option, with no server rendering cost per visitor. This is why frameworks like Next.js, Astro, and Nuxt default marketing/content use cases to static generation with incremental revalidation.

When CSR (or a heavy client app) would be the better call — and why it isn't yours: highly interactive, behind-login, per-user dashboards where SEO is irrelevant and the same shell is reused across many stateful views. A marketing site is the opposite of that profile.

Practical version of the recommendation: static-generate the pages, revalidate on content changes (incremental static regeneration or a rebuild webhook from your CMS), and use client-side JavaScript only for islands of interactivity — nav menus, forms, animations. You get SSR's SEO and speed without paying to render every request live.
T:
Use server-side rendering — and for a content-heavy marketing site specifically, prefer its static flavor (SSG / prerendering at build time) over per-request SSR. Render to HTML on the server; only hydrate the few interactive bits that genuinely need it.

The reasoning:

**SEO is the deciding factor.** Marketing sites live or die by organic search and link previews. With client-side rendering, crawlers and social scrapers receive a near-empty HTML shell and have to execute JavaScript to see your content. Google can do this but does it inconsistently and on a delayed second-pass; most other crawlers and the Open Graph scrapers behind Slack, iMessage, LinkedIn, and X don't run JS at all. Server-rendered HTML puts your copy, headings, and meta tags in the initial response where every crawler reads them reliably.

**First paint is faster, which converts better.** Content-heavy pages are mostly text and images — exactly what HTML is good at delivering immediately. SSR/SSG shows real content on the first byte; CSR shows nothing until the JS bundle downloads, parses, and fetches data, then renders. On marketing pages every extra second of blank screen measurably costs you bounce rate, and Core Web Vitals (LCP in particular) feed back into search ranking.

**The content is the right shape for it.** Marketing content changes when someone edits the CMS, not on every request. That means you can build pages to static HTML and serve them from a CDN — cheapest, fastest, and most resilient option, with no server rendering cost per visitor. This is why frameworks like Next.js, Astro, and Nuxt default marketing/content use cases to static generation with incremental revalidation.

When CSR (or a heavy client app) would be the better call — and why it isn't yours: highly interactive, behind-login, per-user dashboards where SEO is irrelevant and the same shell is reused across many stateful views. A marketing site is the opposite of that profile.

**What would change this recommendation.** Flip toward CSR/client-app rendering if the "marketing site" is actually mostly authenticated, per-user, or real-time interactive content where SEO and shareable link previews don't matter — that's the one condition that overturns the call above. Two softer limits worth naming: if your content is highly personalized or updates many times per minute, pure build-time SSG stops fitting and you'd lean on per-request SSR or edge rendering instead of static files; and if the site is tiny and search traffic genuinely doesn't matter to the business, the SEO argument loses most of its force and the choice comes down to team familiarity. The recommendation assumes the common case: largely public, CMS-driven pages that need to rank and preview well.

Practical version of the recommendation: static-generate the pages, revalidate on content changes (incremental static regeneration or a rebuild webhook from your CMS), and use client-side JavaScript only for islands of interactivity — nav menus, forms, animations. You get SSR's SEO and speed without paying to render every request live.
P:
**Recommendation: render on the server, and for a content-heavy marketing site default to static generation (SSG / build-time prerendering) rather than per-request SSR.** Produce real HTML at build time, serve it from a CDN, and hydrate only the few pieces that are genuinely interactive. Keep per-request SSR in reserve for the handful of pages that truly can't be known at build time (personalized content, search results). You rarely have to pick one mode for the whole site: modern frameworks (Next.js, Astro, Nuxt, SvelteKit) let you choose rendering per route — so set the default to static and opt individual routes up to SSR or down to client-only where each one actually needs it. Client-side rendering as the site-wide default is the one option to rule out.

Why static-first server rendering wins here:

**SEO is the deciding factor.** Marketing sites live or die by organic search and link previews. With client-side rendering, crawlers and social scrapers receive a near-empty HTML shell and must execute JavaScript to see your content. Google can do this, but inconsistently and on a delayed second pass; the Open Graph scrapers behind Slack, iMessage, LinkedIn, and X — and most non-Google crawlers — don't run JavaScript at all, so they see nothing. Server-rendered HTML puts your copy, headings, and meta tags in the initial response, where every crawler reads them reliably.

**Faster first paint, which converts and ranks better.** Content-heavy pages are mostly text and images — exactly what HTML delivers immediately. Static and server rendering put real content in the initial HTML response; client-side rendering shows a blank screen until the JS bundle downloads, parses, fetches data, and renders. On marketing pages, every extra second of blank screen measurably raises bounce rate, and Core Web Vitals — Largest Contentful Paint in particular — feed back into search ranking. Static rendering optimizes the exact metric your traffic depends on.

**The content is the right shape for it.** Marketing content changes when someone edits the CMS, not on every request. That means you can build pages to static HTML and serve them from a CDN — the cheapest, fastest, and most resilient option, with no per-visitor rendering cost and nothing to fall over under a traffic spike. This is why Next.js, Astro, and Nuxt steer content and marketing use cases toward static generation with incremental revalidation by default.

**When client-side rendering is the right call — and why it isn't here.** Heavy client apps make sense for behind-login, per-user dashboards: SEO is irrelevant, first paint matters less than interaction speed once loaded, and the same shell is reused across many stateful views. A public marketing site is the opposite profile on every axis. The trait that justifies CSR — a rich, stateful app behind authentication — is precisely what a marketing site doesn't have.

**What to build.** Static-generate the pages and revalidate on content change (incremental static regeneration, or a rebuild webhook from your CMS). Use client-side JavaScript only for islands of interactivity — nav menus, forms, animations — so the bulk of each page is instant HTML and only the interactive bits ship JS. For the rare page that must be dynamic per request, switch that single route to SSR rather than the whole site. The result is static rendering's SEO and speed everywhere it matters, server rendering where it's genuinely needed, and no cost to render every request live.
